skill.name: baml-integration
skill.description: Guides agents through type-safe BAML schema design, DTO generation, generated client usage, and schema synchronization for LLM features.
skill.summary: Build type-safe BAML workflows for LLM prompts, DTOs, generated clients, and schema sync.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command execution, filesystem, sensitive data, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are primarily Markdown fences, BAML examples, relative imports, and ordinary field names. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it may guide agents to inspect project files and run BAML CLI commands, but no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden network behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Documented BAML CLI and Repository Inspection Commands
low_findings.description: The skill shows shell commands such as find, ls, cat, baml-cli generate, baml-cli check, pip install baml-cli, and git status. These are legitimate development and CI examples, but agents should only run them in trusted repositories and with user awareness.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present in Markdown examples and workflow guidance. They are not malicious, but they are real operational actions an agent could follow.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command detections come from fenced code blocks marked with triple backticks for Python, TypeScript, bash, BAML, YAML, and plain text. These are documentation examples, not Ruby backtick execution or hidden executable payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review confirms the reported locations are Markdown fences and illustrative examples. No executable skill script or dynamic command construction was found.

low_findings.title: Static Filesystem and Sensitive Data Alerts Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are relative TypeScript imports, and the key-file alert points to ordinary example fields such as key_points and annotation keys. No credential files, secret reads, or path traversal logic were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are imports and schema comparison examples, not filesystem traversal or secret access. The surrounding context is ordinary BAML client documentation.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find text telling the evaluator to ignore instructions, override the audit, skip security review, or treat the skill as pre-approved. No evidence found for prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found workflow instructions and documentation, not evaluator-directed override language. Confidence is high for reviewed files but not absolute because all natural language can require human judgment.

content.value_statement: LLM features often break when prompts and output schemas drift from application code. This skill provides BAML patterns for typed prompts, generated DTOs, client wrappers, and schema synchronization.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to define BAML classes, enums, unions, and typed LLM functions.
Shows when to run BAML client generation after schema changes.
Provides Python and TypeScript examples for generated DTO usage.
Describes wrapper patterns for retries, caching, observability, and rate limiting.
Documents schema synchronization patterns for database, API, and frontend types.
Includes reference patterns for clients, fallbacks, validation, and common anti-patterns.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include runnable automation scripts.
Examples use generic domains and must be adapted to each project schema.
It assumes BAML tooling and generated clients are already installed or available.
It does not validate provider credentials, billing settings, or production deployment safety.

use_cases.title: Add Typed LLM Features
use_cases.description: Define BAML request and response types before adding a new LLM-powered workflow.

use_cases.title: Keep Generated Clients Current
use_cases.description: Regenerate Python or TypeScript clients after schema changes and verify type alignment.

use_cases.title: Standardize LLM Access
use_cases.description: Wrap generated BAML clients with retries, caching, metrics, and consistent error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Find Existing BAML Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this repository for BAML files and generated clients. Summarize the current layout and the next safe setup step.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Typed LLM Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a BAML type design for this LLM workflow. Include input classes, output classes, enums, and clear field descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan DTO Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed BAML schema change. Explain which generated DTOs and consumers need updates in Python and TypeScript.

prompt_templates.title: Audit BAML Client Wrapper
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this BAML client wrapper for retries, caching, logging, rate limiting, and error handling. Recommend focused improvements.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to add a typed summary feature with BAML.
output_examples.output: A concise plan naming the input type, output type, function boundary, generation step, and caller update points.

output_examples.input: A team changed database fields and wants BAML schemas to stay aligned.
output_examples.output: A synchronization checklist covering source-of-truth review, BAML type edits, client generation, and verification.

output_examples.input: A platform engineer wants a safer generated client interface.
output_examples.output: A wrapper design covering retry policy, cache keys, metrics labels, rate limits, and structured error returns.

best_practices: Define explicit BAML input and output types before writing prompt logic.
Regenerate clients after schema changes and update all typed consumers together.
Add field descriptions for values that need format, range, or semantic constraints.
anti_patterns: Returning unstructured strings when a typed BAML response is expected.
Editing generated clients by hand instead of updating BAML source files.
Letting database, API, frontend, and BAML schemas drift independently.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps agents apply BAML patterns for typed LLM prompts, DTOs, client generation, and schema synchronization.

faq.question: Does it execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands such as BAML generation and checks that an agent or developer may choose to run.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The examples focus on generated Python and TypeScript clients from shared BAML schemas.

faq.question: Can it replace project-specific schema design?
faq.answer: No. It provides generic patterns that must be adapted to each application domain.

faq.question: Does it include production reliability guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers wrapper patterns for retries, fallback clients, caching, rate limiting, metrics, and structured errors.

faq.question: Is it tied to a specific AI tool?
faq.answer: No. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support, and the guidance is framework agnostic.
